title: He Kākano Ahau: I Am a Seed - Ngā Mahi ā-Rohe o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | The Community Actions of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore the historical and contemporary significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) to Ngāti Kahungunu. This awa has been a vital source of mahinga kai (food gathering), transport, and spiritual significance for generations. The lesson connects to the pūrākau (legend) of Hinekorako, a tupuna (ancestor) who transformed into the stream to provide for her people, embodying the values of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) and manaakitanga (care for others).
learningObjectives: Analyze how the historical uses of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (e.g., as a mahinga kai, transport route) have shaped the identity and practices of the local Ngāti Kahungunu community.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current community-led initiatives (e.g., stream clean-up groups, native planting projects) in addressing environmental challenges facing the awa.

Collaboratively design and present a detailed proposal for a new community action project that applies mātauranga Māori (Māori knowledge) and social studies concepts to enhance the health and mauri (life force) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero: A Walk, A Story
activities.description: Students embark on a guided hīkoi (walk) along a section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua (elder) or representative from Manaaki Te Awa Trust. The guide will share pūrākau (stories), point out historical sites of significance (e.g., old pā sites, former mahinga kai locations), and discuss observed changes to the awa over time. Students will use field journals to sketch, note key points, and record questions.

activities.title: Te Āhua o Te Awa: The State of the Stream
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will conduct a practical environmental investigation of the stream. Using provided water testing kits, they will measure key indicators of stream health (pH, nitrate levels, clarity). They will also conduct a macroinvertebrate survey (using kick nets and identification charts) to assess biodiversity. Data is recorded and compared against historical data provided by the Hawke's Bay Regional Council.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero ā-Hapori: Community Voices
activities.description: Students will prepare for and conduct interviews (in person or via video call) with key community stakeholders. This includes a representative from the Manaaki Te Awa restoration group, a local farmer from a catchment property, a member of the Hastings District Council parks team, and a kaumātua. Questions will focus on their perspectives, roles, and visions for the future of the awa.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Tahi: Collaborative Visioning
activities.description: Using all gathered data, stories, and interview insights, groups will synthesize their findings to identify the most pressing issue facing the awa (e.g., sediment runoff, invasive weeds, reduced native fish populations). They will brainstorm potential community-led action projects that address this issue, considering both mātauranga Māori and modern scientific approaches.

activities.title: He Mahere Mahi: Action Plan Presentation
activities.description: Student groups will formalize their best idea into a detailed action plan proposal. They will create a digital or physical presentation (e.g., slideshow, poster, model) outlining their project's goals, required resources, steps for implementation, and expected outcomes for the awa and community. Groups will present their proposals to the class and a invited guest from the Manaaki Te Awa Trust for feedback.

resources: A kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu or a representative from the Manaaki Te Awa Stream Care Group
The NIWA 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and digital macroinvertebrate identification tool
Historical photographs and land use maps of the Mangakōtukutuku catchment from the Knowledge Bank of Hawke's Bay (Tātauranga o Te Matau-a-Māui)
The Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Tukituki Catchment Proposed Plan Change 6' documentation relevant to water quality

tasks: An individual field journal from the hīkoi, demonstrating active listening, observation, and reflection on the cultural and historical significance of the awa.
A group-produced environmental data report, accurately presenting and interpreting the findings from the stream health investigation.
A collaboratively designed and presented 'Community Action Plan' proposal for enhancing the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges (Place and Environment)
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies (The Economic World)
Social Studies L4: Understand how people define and seek human rights (Identity, Culture, and Organisation)
Social Studies L4: Understand how formal and informal groups make decisions that impact on communities (Continuity and Change)
